methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $246M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 5 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Chips & Semiconductors. Range: $529M - $5.0B.

private.name: d-Matrix
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series C), post-money $2B

private.name: DEEPX
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series C), post-money $529M

data.narrative:
FuriosaAI is a Series C-stage semiconductor design company founded by June Paik that has raised $246M to date. The valuation analysis draws from a peer universe of 11 comparable companies across public and private markets—3 public firms (ALAB, NVDA, AMD) and 8 private semiconductor companies at similar or adjacent funding stages, including d-Matrix, Tenstorrent, Etched, Positron AI, and DEEPX.

Two triangulation methods were applied. The stage-progression method uses the $246M in total capital raised as a valuation proxy and applies a 1.7x step-up multiplier typical for Series C advancement, implying a valuation of $418M. The peer-median method examines five same-sector, same/adjacent-stage comparables and derives a median valuation of $2.0B, with a range spanning $529M (DEEPX, Series C) to $5.0B (Etched, Series B). These two approaches produced a triangulated range from $355M (low) to $2.3B (high), with a median estimate of $1.2B.

The wide spread between methods reflects meaningful uncertainty around FuriosaAI's relative positioning within the frontier-chip market. The stage-progression method may underweight recent momentum in semiconductor valuations, while the peer-median approach captures market multiples but introduces sensitivity to the choice of comparables. Key uncertainties include product-market fit maturity, revenue trajectory, and whether the company's technology focus commands premium or discount multiples relative to the peer set. The $1.2B median represents the central point supported by current data.
